sentences.text: Yeah, it was from there, we, we moved on.
And then we was in a field.
We could see Hamburg burning in the distance.
This was on a Friday afternoon.
With the [INAUDIBLE].
I stayed with them the whole time.
How many-- it must have been about 120, like the whole company.
But it broken down to units.
Sometimes you'd be in threes.
You'd get posted someplace.
And there would only be three in the units.
But usually the tour of duty-- two hours on, four hours off.
And that would go on for 24 hours a day.
You get split up.
It depends what was required.
At the moment, we was all together, waiting.
Like AA duties.
Yes, yes, keeping control of [INAUDIBLE].
Of course the lesson was learned-- Montgomery learned the lesson.
At the time of the Dunkirk retreat, there was chaos on the roads.
Nobody knew where they were going.
And there was no order-- people, you know, free-for-all.
So the first thing, as soon as he come back, he decided you got to have some sort of discipline on the roads.
Bad parking-- you get the same thing today-- parking and overtaking and laying out one-way roads.
Of course, when we was in Holland, the locals said that the roads-- there was no motorways there-- the roads were small roads.
And they got frozen up in the winter.
And they said, if you use the heavy traffic in the winter, when they thaw in March, they'll all be cracked.
You won't be able to use them.
So we had to lay on diversions going up one way and coming back another way to save the, the roads for the push in the spring.
And-- So you were just saying about you were watching Hamburg-- Yeah, we was in the field watching Hamburg burning at the time.
This was on a Friday.
Then a message comes through-- the British Second Army's come across a concentration camp in a place called Belsen.
No one ever heard of it.
So I said, well, what's going on there?
They said they want 17 volunteers to go there.
So I said, the only thing you volunteer for is to go home.
Because if volunteers are wanted, there's an element of danger somewhere.
It's a sicky job.
So what, what, what is it?
Well, there's a lot of dead people there.
There's thousands of dead people.
Like the leaves of autumn fall to the ground, so people are falling down on the ground, dead.
They're just dying off.
They just lay in the road.
And they, they die.
There's thousands of people.
There's-- typhus is running right.
Dysentery is running right.
TB is running right.
And we've got to get some law and order, try and get something put together.
So that was on a Friday.
So they want 17 volunteers.
So I thought to myself, well, if I don't go, who else should go?
Did they identify clearly that any of them were Jewish?
Or was there any mention of that at all?
Yeah, they said-- I said, well, who are these people?
They said, well, the bulk of them are Jewish.
But there was a, a sprinkling of gypsies-- they come from Hungary-- and some Russian prisoners of war.
But the bulk of it was Jewish people.
What we're going to find there?
No, not to that extent.
No, no, no.
No, I had a cousin.
He-- I'd never seen him, but he lives in Paris.
But going through, I had the chance to go to Paris.
So I went to see him.
He sent his wife and his son, one boy, to Vichy.
They registered as Catholics.
So they were left alone.
One son was a doctor, so he went off to the American army.
And he was left in Paris in his flat the whole war by himself.
And it was his neighbors that looked after him, never gave him away.
He was there the whole war.
No, not, not on that scale, not on the scale as it was.
I didn't come across any Jewish people, apart from the one in Brussels.
I didn't know anybody.
And, I mean, you can't walk about saying in the square at night, though, and say, are you Jewish?
Are you Jewish?
No, no.
We didn't come across it, no.
No, no, only that sign in Brussels where it said-- but the Germans put up, not the Belgians.
The Germans put it up.
We got that taken off.
But the, the order come through Friday.
And we moved off on Sunday afternoon.
The army, they take their time.
You know how they are.
They had to send an advance party to find the billet and sort out where we're going.
And we, we got there-- we got there on a Sunday afternoon.
And you see people lying in-- on bunks in these hut sort of things, and all sorts of things.
And the first thing Monday morning, we all had to get a boost of a typhus.
It was done by German nurses.
The first-- when they were told they got to help out with the--
Yeah, this was in the training place.
The first-- when, when they were told they got to help out on, on what's going on in the camp, they thought, you know, it's kind of small thing, you know, a bit of fun.
But when they saw what was going on, they changed their-- they changed their minds over it.
Because they didn't know actually what was going on in the camp.
Because once a person went into a concentration camp, they never come out.
And no one was allowed in.
So no one actually knew what was going on.
The only clue you had is, as you approach the camp-- say, a half a mile from the camp-- you can smell the dead.
You can--
It's some very bad smell.
You just, like, followed the smell.
And it, it led you into the camp.
And the-- when I wrote home at the time-- I remember writing home.
I said, the-- what was going on.
I said, there's no adjective in any language which could possibly describe what was going on in the camp there.
And so we got there Sunday, Monday.
And then we tried to sort out the--
Oh, we went by transport with jeeps.
We all had jeeps.
It was one unit of 17 with a cook.
There were 16 of us.
And the cook was 17.
All military police, yeah.
Well, we didn't know what we was going to find.
All we knew is there's a lot of dead people there.
But we didn't there were people, more or less, as Captain Hardman said, they were 99% gone.
That's why some British officers said, there's, there's no point in trying to save these people.
But he argued, well, you got to do what we can.
We've got to save them.
And we was fortunate-- you call it fortunate-- that this was the 15th of April.
Had it taken place five days later, and the temperature would have been two degrees higher, there would have been a serious outbreak of cholera.
And there was nothing we could do.
The troops would have been withdrawn.
The camp would have been shut.

And one officer was heard to remark-- I didn't hear it, but someone told me-- he said, had he known what he was going to find in the camp, he would have ordered the troops to mark time for five days outside the camp.
There'd be no survivors and no problems.
And Captain Hardman was there.
He was there.
After two weeks, he-- we got the boot-- he, he was ordered to leave.
So he said he's not leaving.
And he was told.
He's a British army party, paid for British taxpayers' money.
He's not a civilian party.
So he said, if you want me, you'll have to come and take me.
I'm not leaving.
And I was the only one that knew what he looked like.
I they knew I was going to say, even if I didn't know where he-- I knew where it was.
But the-- I wasn't going to say where he was.
They were like wooden gates that was open.
Yes, no, but the gates, I think, were shut.
Because no one was allowed out.
Because if they went out, they would spread typhus.
So for-- not because they didn't want them to go out, but for medical reasons.
That's where we took over, the military police, yeah.
Yeah.
Nobody.
It was a free-for-all sort of thing.
But they were too weak to walk.
Nobody could walk or anything.
Well, you couldn't believe what you were-- what you were seeing.
You couldn't believe it.
You didn't know where to look first.
Well, there's people lying in these bunks, in these huts, lying on the floor, lying of top of dead bodies, bodies lying all over the show.
And they were all looking at you as if to say, you know, you're-- they couldn't believe what we were doing there.
They didn't, didn't know what was going on.
What, the people, they were--
They didn't have their faculties with them.
There were a few, a few girls at where we had the information desk.
So we had a, a girl come in.
She used to spray you.
If you wanted to go into the concentration camps, she used to spray them with this DDT, like you shouldn't get with lice.
And she, she was reasonably dressed.
And she had nail varnish.
And she didn't have her hair shaved.
And she looked-- she didn't look too bad.
So I said to her, I said that, how is it that you don't look so bad?
You look reasonable.
You got your hair.
You got cosmetics.
Well, where did you get it from?
She says, look, I'm 21 years old.
I've lost all my family.
I've got no one.
She said, the only way you can stay alive-- if you were fortunate, and you lived with an SS officer, you had something to eat.
Otherwise, you, you, you'd finish up in the gas chambers.
So she said, well, what should I do?
She said, that's the only way you could have stayed alive.
And the funny thing is-- the, the Germans have got these funny ways.
There was plenty of German Frauleins about.
But they had to have Jewish girls for mistresses.
And this was portrayed in where Steven Spielberg showed that the camp commandant, Goeth, as much as he hated the Jews, he had a Jewish mistress.
Well, the people, when they saw us, when they saw it was, you know, military police, they, they took the view, well, there can't be much difference than the German police.
You know, the police was the police wherever he is.
So they shied away.
That they didn't want nothing to do with us.
And then called some of them over and asked them what's going on.
And when you start speaking Yiddish to them, they start looking.
Oh, the policeman speaks Yiddish.
So they started getting a bit more friendly.
And then, before the week was out, we was a unit of 17 with a cook.
But we was drawing rations for 35 people, because some of the fellows, they all wanted an interpreter.
And some had two interpreters attached to them.
So we were drawing rations for them.
But one of them said-- she's got a little boy, a cousin of 10.
She says, she can't leave him on his own.
He's, he's got to come with us.
So we were drawing rations for him as well.
So we were drawing rations for 35 people.
We was a unit of 17.
No, they were, they were pleasant.
They were all young girls, who possibly living with the SS.
So they had a bit of clothes and they didn't look too bad.
They wasn't-- well, I wouldn't say they were, wasn't-- they were underweight.
But they didn't have their hair shaved off or anything like that.
No, well, you see, the medical units start coming in.
And then the chief of the-- the medical staff used to have a meeting every evening, what to do for the following day, how to proceed.
And the chief of the-- senior medical officer, Brigadier Glyn Hughes, he was in charge of everything.
And all credit goes to him.
And also, you see, they was that far gone, you couldn't give them a proper meal to eat.
They hadn't eaten for years.
So they were given a slice of bread and, and very gradually tried to get-- and the people were saying, well, they're not much different from the Germans.
Look what they're giving us to eat.
They're not giving us anything to eat.
But they couldn't eat.
You couldn't give them.
Some people found something to eat.
They, they didn't live, because they wasn't used to eating a proper meal.
No, it was a mixture.
There was one man come up to me.
He said, how can I get back to London?
I said, I don't know.
When it comes to that, I said, I'd also like to get back to London.
[LAUGHS]
I said, tell me something.
Where did you learn to speak such good English?
He says, I was born in London.
I said, well, what are you doing here?
He said he was in the cigar business.
And he was in Holland buying cigars or whatever it was.
And it got overrun by the Germans.
And he got taken prisoner.
And he finished there.
And he said, look here's my birth certificate.
He was born in [INAUDIBLE], as it was.
And he named me the street, [?
Floristan ?] Street, opposite [?
Steppenagreen ?] Station.
I knew it well.
So he said, I got caught up with it.
I, I'm, I come from London.
And I didn't see him anymore.
I don't know what, what happened to him, whether he got back to London.
Some of the inmates, they went back to Poland.
I didn't see them for about three weeks.
I said, where have you been?
She said that they went back to Poland.
Well, how did you get there?
You didn't walk from Germany.
Well, the train came.
We went on a train.
They didn't pay.
They went to Poland.
There was nothing there.
So they said, at least we get something to eat here.
So we come back.
And one girl came up to me in the information room.
And she said she remembers having an aunt and uncle in Palestine.
How can she get a letter to them?
Because there was no postal service.
She didn't have an address.
She didn't have a, a piece of writing paper and an envelope to write with.
Ball pens wasn't invented in those days.
[LAUGHS] They was, actually.
The RAF used them for high altitude, because ink in the pen would freeze at high altitude.
So they come up with a ball pen.
But that wasn't used in commercial bases.
So I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, how do you, how do you get a letter to Palestine?
So I said, look, I'll tell you what I'll do.
I'll give you a piece of paper and a pencil.
Write what you want to write.
And I'll write the address on.
And I'll send it through the army post-- postal service.
Of course, the army postal service was very good.
I said, I don't promise you the letter will get there.
But I'll put it through the post office-- the postal service.
If it gets there, it gets there.
And I said, don't come back for at least a week.
Because it has to go to Palestine and come back.
It has to go through the censor.
And we'll see.
So I gave her some paper and the pencil.
She wrote a letter.
I wrote the address on.
So I looked at the letter, thinking she'd write in German and Polish.
Well, no, she's got it written in Yiddish.
So what the censor saw-- made of it when he looked at it, [LAUGHS] it's written in Yiddish.
Anyway, she-- after a week, she came every day, nothing, nothing.
And one day, I got a reply.
And I stood by the door.
And I knew she was waiting for it.
And I saw her in the distance.
And I waved her this letter.
I says. ?] And she, she sprinted like an Olympic athlete the last few yards.
And she jumped on me.
And she hugged me.
And she kissed me.
And that was-- what was it I said-- that was a military police's finest moment.
And I go to her.
I said, what-- I suppose her aunt and uncle were very pleased they heard from her.
And least she got a, an answer.
So it did go through the postal service.
She must be about 21, 22.
Channah, but nobody could pronounce it.
They actually called her 'Cana,' 'Cana,' 'Chana.' They couldn't pronounce Channah.
Her name was Channah.
She came from Poland, actually.
No, they all disappeared.
And then one day, I had to escort-- I had to go pick up two Russian prisoners that were accused of looting.
And looting was a serious crime, even in Germany.
But in Germany, you was allowed to requisition anything.
You wanted a requisition-- Montgomery issued an order-- he used to give orders [INAUDIBLE].
He said, up to now, you've been liberated.
Every country you've gone into, you've acted liberators.
Now you've gone into Germany, you go in as conquerors.
Therefore, there's no excuse for any man under my command not to have a decent bed at night.
Consequently, all tents will be sent back to headquarters forthwith.
And when he said forthwith, he didn't mean you looked up-- put on a pair of kid gloves, look up the Marquess of Queensberry rules of warfare, and see what the next move is.
When he says forthwith, he meant there and then.
So that, that more or less gave you-- you can requisition what you like.
But these two people, they said they, they were accused of looting two chickens.
So I had to go pick them up.
I went into the-- they were still being held in the concentration camp.
There was stone walls, a massive stone door, like a dungeon.
It was built like a dungeon.
As you came in, there, there was some huts there.
There was this-- these few cells, which they--
They're cells to put people in.
And I couldn't have been in there more than 10 minutes, a quarter of an hour at the most.
I took them back to the courts.
The courts went on for about a good few hours.
And then eventually, they found that everybody in court spoke French.
So they won't need a Russian interpreter.
Everybody spoke French.
And they were told, you mustn't loot from the Germans.
You can requisition, but you mustn't loot.
So after that, I went to have a haircut.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the night, I felt an irritation in my back and my chest.
I thought it was from the haircut-- the hair gets-- so I brushed it down, you know?
So I put the light on.
I was covered in lice.
And I was only there ten minutes at court and went out.
And that was the conditions of the camp.
And then, another time, one of the fellows came.
And he was an old soldier.
He was in the First War.
So he knew more than we did.
He came in one morning at breakfast.
He's got a ring on one finger.
He's got a ring on another finger.
He's got a wristwatch on this hand and a wristwatch on that hand, fountain pens in here.
So I called him aside.
I said, where did you get it all?
He couldn't have bought it anywhere.
There's nowhere to buy it.
And he wouldn't have stolen it from anybody.
He said, shh.
Meet me at the main gate, 7 o'clock.
So I couldn't wait until it was 7 o'clock in the evening.
I went to the main gate.
He took us across the road.
There was an ordinance depot.
And there was someone in charge there.
He said, you got 20 minutes.
The orderly officer comes at 20 past 7.
So you got 20 minutes.
Take what you like.
We went in.
There was boxes stuck to the ceiling-- must've been dozens of boxes, like egg-- from apples, apple boxes.
And they were stuffed with wage envelopes.
And each wage envelope had a item of jewelry or watches.
And on the envelope was the name and the address, I think it was, and the date of birth for the person who it was taken from.
And they, they put these in the boxes, giving the people the impression that they're going to get it back one day.
But they had no intention of giving back.
But it shows you how thorough the Germans were.
And we, we're opening these envelopes.
And all they had, the-- as far as the good watches-- was gone.
There was pocket watches, hunter watches.
They were all hunter watches.
But I come across a few watches.
One was a Longines.
I knew a Longines or Omega was a good watch.
And I took a couple of watches.
And while I was doing this, a fellow came in, all excited.
And he shouted, it's all over!
It's all over!
The war's all over.
Germany is going to sign unconditional surrender tomorrow afternoon.
And there ?] [? will be ?] [? peace. ?] And no one lifted their head up to say anything.
See, everybody carried on looking, that they were-- see what they could find in these watches.
There was hundreds there.
And then, when we went out, somebody told us, someone just came and said, oh, the war's over.
It's all finished.
But we said-- we couldn't wear those watches because knowing where they come from in Plaszow, you couldn't possibly wear them.
So we just took them as souvenirs as if to say, but if we don't take them, somebody else will come along and take them.
So we took a few each.
But I've still-- I gave a few-- I donated to the Sternberg Centre here in London.
And one, I've still got, a pocket watch.
It's a stopwatch, does perfect time.
I've still got that.
Well, the-- mainly, the information room, and also patrols at night.
The curfew was 11:00.
At ten to 11:00, you can be sure you wouldn't see one German in the street.
There wasn't one German.
They were all off the streets.
But the inmates, they were walking about till 1, 2 o'clock in the morning.
I said, don't you know it's-- 11 o'clock is curfew?
We've been locked up long enough.
Well, I suppose you're right there.
So they walked about till 1, 2 o'clock in the morning.
They had nowhere to go, nothing to do.
But the Germans, about ten till 11:00, you wouldn't see one on the road.
They're very strict on that.
Did you get involved in any way of helping other inmates?
Well, in the information, if they'd come in-- oh, then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the clothing.
These people, they were walking about in these striped pajamas.
And we had to get clothing for them.
Oh, it was a good few miles.
It was a Good few hours driving.
He went into Berlin, went into the big stores, like Harrods or Selfridges.
And they just cleared the shelves.
And then Montgomery's sister-- Lady Mountbatten, she was commandant of the Red Cross-- they took a big hangar where they used to keep the tanks.
And they nicknamed it Harrods and started distributing the clothing, to try and clothe the people.
So I think he made two or three trips like that, to try and give somebody a pair of shoes and a dress or something to wear.
They were walking about in these pajamas.
I don't [? remember-- ?] till about June.
It took about a good six weeks, two months.
And then one day, I was on duty in the information-- from the information desk.
A woman came in.
She said, there's an SS officer walking down the road, going out.
From our information building to the main gate must have been about a 20-minute walk.
Because the Germans, they like big buildings, big pathways, big roads.
She said, there's an SS officer down the road.
I says, yeah, but what did she do?
She said, she used to make the selections.
When people come off the transports in Auschwitz, she used to stand there with a whip.
And she used to pick out, you live and you die.
You go to the left.
You go to the right.
She used to choose who goes-- who lives and who dies.
I said, yeah, and did you see her do it?
She said, no.
Do you know anyone that saw her do it?
She said, no.
I said, well, there's nothing I can do.
The whole conversation's in Yiddish.
Because I didn't speak Polish or German.
This is all in Yiddish.
So it come in handy sometimes.
So I said, well, I'm sorry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE]
So I said, well, there's nothing I can do.
Hearsay evidence is not admissible in an English court of law.
There's nothing I can do.
She said, but everyone knows she done it.
She's an-- I says, well, 'everyone knows' is not good enough.
I've got to have two, two-- I must have two witnesses that saw her actually do it.
Yes.
What, who, the SS officer?
That was Irma Grese.
So I said, I must have-- even if she did do it-- I must have, I must have two witnesses that actually saw her do it.
Otherwise, what will happen, if it goes to court, even if we took her in, if it goes to court, it'll get thrown out.
And once it gets thrown out, you can't charge her again for the same thing.
So she got down on her hands and knees.
And she started crying.
The tears were running down her cheeks.
She says, I beg of you, as one Jew to another.
So I said, look, hold it, hold it.
I took-- picked her up.
I put her in the chair.
I says, now tell me, there's no one at all?
She said, the people that used to work in the crematoriums, they were only there for three months.
After three months, they were sent to the gas chambers, there shouldn't be any witnesses.
So I said to the corporal standing next to me, I says, look, there's only one way you're going to get her out of this office.
She's very persistent, I says.
Take an armed guard and a jeep and bring her in.
So at least you'll get rid of her out of the office.
Because she, she wouldn't go out.
They said, look, if there's no witnesses, you're not going to get anywhere.
You're better off if you leave her go.
And maybe at a later date, someone might recognize her.
I said, look, I don't usually ask you to do me any favors.
But this time, I'm asking.
Take the jeep with two armed men and bring her in.
All right.
So all of a sudden, the hunter became the hunted.
So she went in, this woman.
She-- took her in the jeep.
So she was so proud as a peacock she was in the jeep, going after this SS officer.
She picked her up.
We brought her in.
Where was she living, the SS officer?
How come she was so close to the camp?
She must have been living outside.
I don't know what, what she was doing there.
I might have a vague idea.
I'll come to that later.
So we brought her in.
She looked like a film star-- blond hair, blue eyes, like made to measure for the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she, she came in.
And I said, can I see your pass?
So she shows me a pass signed by the camp commandant giving the permission to go to [?
Seelow, ?] to travel [INAUDIBLE].
Because she-- you had to, to go from one village to the other, you had to have a permit.
The Germans had to have a permit to go from one place to the other.
So I said, no, I don't want that.
I want your SS card, [GERMAN], what they used to call it, [GERMAN].
So she gave me the [GERMAN].
And it was about as big as a credit card-- a little bit bigger than a credit card.
Name, Irma Grese, [?
Ober ?] [?
Lieutenant, ?] born in 1923, I think she was.
So I said, hmm, I says, well, but we better keep her.
Because there was a regulation that all prisoners of war could be detained for 48 hours in transit.
And you give them a tin of corned beef, like, for the transit.
I said, well, we'll keep her for 48 hours and see if there-- there must be someone somewhere, all these thousands of people.
So she said, could she go back to her home to collect a few personal belongings, like a change of clothing, a comb, a brush, cosmetics.
She wasn't short of anything.
I'll take the receiver off.
